elements.section-2411-002: Metric space of measures with respect to the Wasserstein distance
elements.sent-2411-146: The overall idea of the current approach is to treat the task of sampling from the target distribution or to approximate it with a tractable model as a minimization of a certain functional, that quantifies the dissimilarity to the target.
elements.sent-2411-147: We denote the functional $\mathcal{E}$ and in what follows fix it to be the $\operatorname{KL}$ divergence: \begin{equation}E(\rho) = \operatorname{KL}(\rho | \rho_\infty) = \int\log\left( \frac{\rho}{\rho_\infty}\right)d\rho.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-148: This functional is of particular importance because the unnormalized density is used in its computation instead of the true one.
elements.sent-2411-149: Since the value of the functional would only change by a constant the minimizer stays unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-150: In the sequel, however, we would use the $\mathcal{E}$ notation for the functional, except for explicitly mentioned parts, to signify that the proximal method can be used for a variety of functionals (as long as the aforementioned parts can be efficiently tackled numerically).
elements.sent-2411-151: Probability measures lack a natural linear space structure.
elements.sent-2411-152: However, if a metric structure is imposed, an analog of a steepest descent can be defined, either as a discrete sequence of proximal steps (minimizing movements) or as a continuous process (a gradient flow).
elements.sent-2411-153: The Wasserstein distance is based on the solution of an optimal transport problem.
elements.sent-2411-154: Unlike other metrics defined on probability measures such as Hellinger or total variance (TV), it is takes into account the metric of the underlyig space, thus being <<aware>> of its the topology.
elements.sent-2411-155: Additionally, transport maps that are optimal with respect to this distance can be shown to exist under certain conditions.
elements.sent-2411-156: This motivates numerous applications of the Wasserstein distance, both as an element of the analysis in PDE theory (e.g.~\cite{jordan1998variational,otto2001geometry,liero2013gradient,craig2017nonconvex and references therein) and as a practical tool in statistics~\cite{bernton2019statistical , machine learning~\cite{torres2021survey , image processing~\cite{bonneel2023survey and others.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-322: This section is concerned with the numerical assessment of the method proposed above.
elements.sent-2411-323: First, the properties of the method are explored with a trivial example of fitting a Gaussian target with diagonal covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-324: The dependence of the convergence properties of the fixed-point iteration on the stepsize and regularization parameter is studied.
elements.sent-2411-325: We demonstrate that in principle a single proximal step can be sufficient to provide a good approximation.
elements.sent-2411-326: This comes at the cost of increasing complexity of the fixed-point iteration, which in turn can be mitigated by implementing accelerated fixed-point schemes.
elements.sent-2411-327: Second, the method is tested against the baseline Metropolis-Hastings MCMC method on several synthetic problems.
elements.sent-2411-328: We highlight the performance of the method in case of multimodal distributions and distributions with nonconvex potentials, which pose well-known challenges for the baseline method.
elements.sent-2411-329: We carefully estimate the <<computational budget>> of our method, expressed as the number of the calls to the posterior, and demonstrate that our posterior approximation is superior to the baseline given the same amount of posterior calls.
elements.sent-2411-330: For the Python implementation, the teneva package~\cite{chertkov2023black is employed, which contains all basic tensor-train operations as well as a cross-approximation solver.}
elements.sent-2411-331: Before providing the numerical applications of the described method, we briefly discuss the approaches used to comprehensively assess its practical performance.

elements.sent-2411-432: The present section consists of case studies for two certain PDE-constrained Bayesian inverse problem.
elements.sent-2411-433: We consider the application of our method for Bayesian inversion.
elements.sent-2411-434: We assess our method in the context of estimating the unknown model parameters while providing an uncertainty quantification.
elements.sent-2411-435: We also demonstrate how the fitted TT model for the Bayesian posterior can be re-used to efficiently solve an associated problem.
elements.sent-2411-436: In our case, importance sampling for some quantity of interest non-trivially depending on the unknown parameters is considered.
elements.sent-2411-437: Specifically, we aim to reconstruct the initial condition in an initial value problems for PDEs.
elements.sent-2411-438: Our two test cases are described in the following.
elements.sent-2411-439: The initial value problem for the wave equation is considered.
elements.sent-2411-440: It is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:ip_hyperbolic_pde} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = 0, u(0, x) = h_\theta(x),\ \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}(0, x) = 0,\end{equation} where the initial condition depends on the unknown parameters $\theta$, the position of <<peaks>> in the initial distribution \begin{equation}h_\theta(x) = \sum\limits_{i=1}^d e^{-(x - \theta_i)^2}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-474: To briefly summarize the idea of importance sampling, given a certain quantity of interest $F$ for which $\mathbb{E _{\rho_\infty [F]$ has to be estimated, one designs a distribution $\rho_F$ and a weighting function $w$ such that $\mathbb{E _{\rho_F [wF] = \mathbb{E _{\rho_\infty [F]$ and the variance is minimized.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-508: Deep generative models have become extremely popular in the field of image and video generation.
elements.sent-2411-509: Such tasks are very high-dimensional in nature and the underlying considered distribution mapping usually is rather complicated.
elements.sent-2411-510: After the success of GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks) in recent years, methods based on gradient flows can now be considered state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-511: These can be inherently deterministic or stochastic as e.g. described in~\cite{song2020score , where an ODE based measure transport is derived as analog process (in terms of the obtained marginal densities) of an SDE based (reversed) diffusion.}
elements.sent-2411-512: When dealing with parametric PDEs as in this project, normalizing flows (NF) turn out to be a viable subclass with beneficial properties for robust training, inversion and conditioning on measurement data.
elements.sent-2411-513: The classical and rather general approach using Neural ODEs~\cite{chen2018neural can be generalized further by introducing stochastic layers into the measure transport map represented by a deep neural network.}
elements.sent-2411-514: This allows for accurate approximations of complicated nonlinear and multimodal densities, see~\cite{hagemann2021generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-515: In principle, different types of stochastic enhancement layers are possible that can e.g. rely on Langevin dynamics, the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm, variational auto-encoders or diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-516: In preparation of our method, we examined acceleration methods for the simulation of Langevin processes in~\cite{eigel2022less .}
elements.sent-2411-517: There, two ideas were introduced to foster faster convergence of the considered particle ensemble.
elements.sent-2411-518: First, successive particle enrichments are proposed, reducing the number of possibly costly evaluations of the forward model.
elements.sent-2411-519: Second, an homotopy approach interpolating between a simple and the true Bayesian potential guiedes the particles in particular to different modes of the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-520: NFs are used to model a data distribution $P_\mathcal Y$ as the push-forward of a simple (typically standard normal) latent distribution $P_\mathcal Z$, given as a diffeomorphism $\mathcal T_\theta:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb R^d$, which is parametrized by $\theta$.

elements.sent-2411-531: To solve the inverse problem, the transport from the latent distribution $P_\mathcal Z$ to the data distribution $P_\mathcal Y$ has to be conditioned on available measurements to obtain a distribution of the parameters $\mathbf p$.
elements.sent-2411-532: We consider a conditional NF $\mathcal T(y,\cdot)$ that is invertible for $y\in\mathbb R^k$ and call $y$ the condition on $\mathcal T$.
